author: @Simonserfati
text:
L'affaire Daniel Galvan Viña (2013) — "le DanielGate"
Ce ressortissant espagnol avait été condamné au Maroc à 30 ans de prison pour le viol de 11 enfants marocains. Il fut gracié par le roi Mohammed VI.
L'affaire Calvez (2013) — réseau pédophile à Casablanca
Un journaliste français d'une soixantaine d'années, Calvez Yvon Guillaume Albert, dirigeait depuis sa résidence à Casablanca un réseau pédophile avec l'aide de deux femmes de ménage marocaines qui lui fournissaient régulièrement des fillettes âgées d'une dizaine d'années. Certains viols étaient filmés. 
L'affaire Soumère (2006) — Marrakech
Jacques-Henri Soumère, patron de l'opéra de Massy et ex-directeur du théâtre Mogador à Paris, avait été surpris en flagrant délit de pédophilie avec deux jeunes garçons mineurs à Marrakech. Des cassettes pornographiques avaient été saisies. Malgré le flagrant délit, il n'écopa que de quatre mois de prison avec sursis et 500 dirhams d'amende, avant de repartir librement en France.  
L'affaire Philippe Servaty — "le pornographe d'Agadir"
Cet ex-journaliste belge venait fréquemment au Maroc entre 2001 et 2004. À Agadir, il abordait des adolescentes issues de milieux très pauvres, leur promettait une vie meilleure, et les convainquait de se laisser photographier nues dans des positions dégradantes avant de diffuser les images sur internet
L'affaire de l'Espagnol à Errachidia
Un ressortissant espagnol de 38 ans avait loué une maison à Errachidia dans le seul but d'accueillir des mineurs pour les exploiter sexuellement. Il se faisait passer pour le président d'une association de bienfaisance et promettait de faciliter l'émigration en Espagne.  
L'affaire de Jacques Leveugle — Khénifra
Pendant des décennies, ce discret professeur français, investi dans la vie locale à Khénifra, a abusé de nombreux mineurs. C'est en France, et non au Maroc, que le scandale a éclaté : il fut mis en examen pour viols et agressions sexuelles sur 89 garçons âgés de 13 à 17 ans, commis sur plus de cinquante ans dans plusieurs pays, sans qu'aucune procédure judiciaire n'ait été engagée contre lui au Maroc. 
L'affaire des Koweïtiens à Marrakech (2024)
Sept étrangers de nationalité koweïtienne, impliqués dans une affaire de tourisme sexuel à Marrakech, ont pu fuir le Maroc avant même leur procès,
Un problème structurel
Le Maroc est décrit par les organisations de défense des droits de l'enfant comme une destination de plus en plus prisée des pédophiles, en raison de sa proximité avec l'Europe, de la pauvreté de nombreux enfants, et de la faiblesse des structures juridiques. De manière paradoxale, les enfants exploités sont souvent traités comme des délinquants plutôt que des victimes par le code pénal marocain. 
Un schéma récurrent est également dénoncé : les ressortissants étrangers pris en flagrant délit bénéficient de libertés provisoires contre caution et quittent impunément le territoire, tandis que les jeunes filles marocaines impliquées sont, elles, incarcérées.

author: @frankethomas1248
text: Casablanca came out in 1942, which means that much of it was probably shot in 1941. Remember that America entered World War 2 in December of 1941, following the Japanese bombing of Pearl Harbor. And D-day was still  2.5 years away. So at the time this movie was made, and subsequently screened for various audiences, nobody knew how the war would end, or who would win. Under such dark & doleful conditions they made what is arguably the best movie ever. I enjoyed your reaction, ma'am; thank you for sharing this with us!

author: @jrepka01
text:
It's a great story of love and sacrifice. But it's really a story meant to convince the people of the US that entering the war was the right thing to do. The war had been going on in Europe for more than two years while America held back, not entering until Pearl Harbor was attacked in December 1941. 

When we are introduced to Rick in the beginning he is signing a document that is dated December 2 1941, just a few days before the Japanese attack, so the entire film takes place before the US committed to the war. At one point Rick says "I'll bet they're asleep in New York. I'll bet they're asleep all over America” suggesting that Americans remain blissfully unconcerned about the fate of the rest of the world.

The movie was shot in early 1942, after the US had entered the war, and released at the end of that year. It was a great propaganda film, with the story of romance and betrayal playing an equal role to the story of courage and resistance to the fascist occupation. Many of the European actors in the film were only recent arrivals from occupied Europe, some with stories not too dissimilar to the stories in the movie itself. The La Marseillaise scene was particularly potent as none of the participants were "acting" while it was being filmed.

author: @timokaurala6657
text:
This is one of those rare movies that seem to get better every time you watch it. So many details in the lighting and camera work, how tight the plot is, yet how smoothly it seems to unfold, how everything seems to slot in perfectly as it wraps up. You'll watch this years from now and have a whole new appreciation for this beautifully made little movie.
Btw, the actors singing and being emotional in the national anthem scene were in real life refugees from France.  The tears you see in their faces and the emotional way they sang the French national anthem were real.

author: @ToABrighterFuture
text:
One scene that sometimes gets overlooked:  "just a lucky guy."  Which is understandable, because there's math involved.  😱

In roulette, the chips are colored based on who's playing them, with (traditionally) each player setting their own chip value.

Let's assume Jan is betting 5 francs per chip.  At Rick's direction, he bets 22, en plein ("straight up") for 30 francs.  That pays 35 to 1, which is equivalent to 36 for 1.  So his 30 francs are now 1,080 francs.

You'll note that on the second spin, Rick is conspicuously signing something.  That's most likely a limit waiver, because most casinos won't let you bet 216 chips en plein, and viewers at the time probably would have known that.

When that hits, those 1,080 francs become 38,880 francs.

Now, mentally rewind to that early scene in the cafe, where one refugee is trying to get smuggled out on a fishing boat.  The asking price, was 15,000 francs, in cash.

Renault wasn't going to ask them for anything near that.  Let's assume he wanted 20,000 francs for visas for both of them.  That leaves them with 18,880 francs.

Without getting any further into the weeds, I'm going to assume an exchange rate of 80 francs to 1 USD on the black market.  That comes out to about $236 in 1941; adjusting for inflation, that's about $4,500 in 2026.

Not exactly life changing, but enough for them to get on their feet in America.

author: @randybass8842
text: Rick was traumatized by Ilsa not showing up at the train in Paris with no explanation, and hated his memories of that time. Even when Ilsa came into the bar after closing, he did not expect to hear an acceptable reason as to why she left him and insulted her. But when she came to his apartment, she was able to give him an acceptable reason that he could accept that her love for him was genuine at the time and still was. What that did was restore the true love that Rick also had for her and made his memories of Paris a happy time again. So, in the end, when he says, "We'll always have Paris," that shows that he has been able to drop the caustic personality that he had. It restored in him that he had once had true love and lost it through no fault of his or of Ilsa. So it was not necessary to cling to that love,  and he could let her go for the greater good of the war effort.

author: @odysseusrex5908
text:
This is the greatest movie ever made. It is infinitely quotable and infinitely quoted.
The reason Rick was refusing to give them the letters was not that he wanted to keep Ilsa close to him but that he wanted to punish her for seemingly betraying him.
I think you missed what Louis was doing. He was using his position of power to force young women to sleep with him in return for exit visas. That is what he was doing with the young Bulgarian girl. What she wanted to know from Rick was whether Louis would actually give her and her husband their visas if she gave him what he wanted. Rick tells her he always has, but then goes and makes sure they get the money they need so their can be no doubt.
When Louis tells his men to round up the usual suspects, of course he knows who just shot Major Strasser tight in front of his eyes. He wants to cover up that fact and protect his friend. Also, he has just, finally, picked a side. You saw him open and then throw away a bottle of Vichy water. Vichy is a resort town in France known for its mineral springs. It was also the de facto capital of France after the government fled Paris. Throwing away the bottle represented his break with the Vichy government and decision to join the continuing fight against the Nazis.

author: @Jer-7007
text: Well, I'm glad to see that you liked it.  But I doubt if anyone from your generation will understand the political context of the story (which would have been well understood by audiences of the time).  At this time, Casablanca (which is in Morocco) was a French colony.  But Germany had conquered France in WWII.  This meant that France, itself, was under German rule.  But French colonies (like Morocco) were kind of on their own.  That's why the French colonial police chief in this movie (Renault) , wants to enforce French law, but must kind of defer to his German masters - which he opposes sentimentally.  This is a delicate political situation,  well known to people of the time but that most people of this time wouldn't understand.

text:
One thing a lot of people miss is that Yvonne's arc was exactly a precis of Rick's.  Rick jilts her and then says, "I never plan that far ahead," which is the same as Ilsa, who said, "That's too far ahead to plan," the day before she fails to show up at the train station.

And after being jilted by Rick, Yvonne makes a similar decision to him -- she stops caring, puts herself first, and starts playing both sides, even flirting with German officers despite being French.

But Victor Laszlo's gesture, having the band play "La Marseillaise," reminds her of her roots and she rejects the German.

Her own short character arc is a perfect micro-version of Rick's.

author: @kschneyer
text:
A lovely reaction! Thank you so much.

The great fiction writer Elizabeth Bear once told me that Rick is the classic example of a character in conflict with himself. Rick doesn’t understand what he wants. He begins the film believing that he wants to be left alone in his self-protective shell. Then he thinks he wants Ilsa. But at the end, he realizes he wants to be part of something bigger than himself. This was always true of Rick (think about his military and political past), but the pain of losing Ilsa makes him think he can only survive by caring about nothing. It is the return of Ilsa and the meeting of Victor that re-awakens in him who he really is.

author: @garyi.1360
text:
The acting is great by all the cast.  Though what shines most to me is the writing.  The dialogue, as you say, is filled with those classic lines.  They are so perfect you can't believe there was ever any indecision for what the words should be.  And if you watch some of the documentaries about the production you can hear those stories.  One line that I like to point to is in the opening when a narrator sets the scene.  As he ends his part his hand off to the audience is perfect.  His words trail off, "while others wait in Casablanca, and wait, and wait, and wait...". How he says it, long drawn out, with almost boredom, and yet with intrigue is perfectly and exactly how we find the situation in Casablanca.  It's nearly Shakespearean in how well it involves and invites you to watch the tale as it begins.  And the sharp, wittiness and pacing in the story just keeps you enjoying it to the ending.  That as well is a perfect hand off for Rick and Louie's tales to set off into the future and with gladness and optimism.
Do watch it again as the writing just gets richer each time.

author: @lemorab1
text:
A video used to be on YouTube (maybe it was about the making of "Casablanca") that commented on the scene in Rick's Cafe where the camera pans over various refugees trying to pawn their jewelry, etc. to buy their way out of "Casablanca;" trying to buy exit visas, pay smugglers, and hire boats. The video gave the names and backgrounds of these bit players, many of whom had held important jobs in the European film and theater industries, before fleeing for their lives. They lived very hard lives in the United States, never regaining the status and security they had enjoyed before Hitler came to power. Two of the noteworthy players in small supporting roles were Madeleine Le Beau, Rick's sometime girlfriend, and her husband Marcel Dalio, the croupier. They had a harrowing time leaving Europe. Yvonne's passionate singing of La Marseillaise and her flowing tears were not acting, they were real. At the time of filming, S. Z. Sakall (Carl the headwaiter) had three sisters and many other family members in Nazi concentration camps, and he had no idea if they were alive or dead. 

The Epstein brothers created the character of Ilsa Lund, partly based on Spanish painter Remedios Varo, in Marseilles when they were there, torn between two men, and trying to get out of Europe. (I read this in Janet Kaplan's biography of Varo, "Unexpected Journeys.")

The movie never flags, moving along at a good clip, thanks to Director Michael Curtiz.

author: @johnnehrich9601
text:
One of the things to keep in mind is that the VERY restrictive Hays Code was still in effect that censored movies in all sorts of ways.  It was adopted in the early 1930's and was replaced by our current multi-level letter code rating system in 1969  (and an entire decade's worth of zany movies that broke all the rules followed - Blazing Saddles, Airport, Naked Gun, Holy Grail, Life of Brian, Rocky Horror).

In the case of this movie, as long as the husband was still alive, the Code required that she stay with him - adultery of any form was prohibited.  (It is worth looking into the effect of the Code which explains a lot about the movies made during it and those made in the immediate freedom right afterwards.)

Among other things, even the most iconic line, the last one, from the blockbuster 1939 Gone With The Wind, "Frankly my dear, I don't give a damn" required lots of pressure.  The Code required words like "pregnant," and "whore" (and all the variations on this), were NOT allowed. In the classic 1939 Stagecoach, modern audiences can't figure out why one woman is being driven out town (for moral reason) and the other is in the final stage of pregnancy, but the audiences knew exactly what the subtle clues indicated.

Even a married couple could NOT be seen sleeping in a double bed.  Any character who committed a criminal act HAD to be seen getting his just desserts by the end of the film.  Movies could not show any gore - guys got shot and all the audience saw there bloodless round holes.  (And Hitchcock's 1962Psycho really broke a lot of rules - they had to devise a way to show flowing blood because up that point, movies didn't show this.)

author: @steelers6titles
text: Note that Renault is a double-crosser until the end; he phones Strasser instead of the airport, to warn him of the impending escape.  He has a history of corruption,  which includes bribery and offering escape for sex.  Rick, despite his professed cynicism, in reality hates dishonesty and corruption.  Yet he seemingly trusts Renault at the end, which, given the latter's history, is somewhat mystifying.  Rick is no fool.  But the ending fits the film.  We'll never know if the partnership succeeded.

author: @thomastimlin1724
text: So hard to choose, but I will pick "Of all the Gin joints in all the towns in all the world...she walks into mine."  In college around 1978, they played old movies and a bunch of us went to Casablanca. For the next 3 months we were all quoting it and saying certain lines to make each other laugh. One girl, a  vocal major who was a very talented  singer,  in the music  dept where I went, she'd walk into the  student  break room and inevitable some guy would say "Of all the Gin joints in all the towns in all the world...she walks into mine."  She  looked confused. Then one day she  found out what was going on. Soon day she  had  an answer, with  her nose in the  air: "If had known you were here, I wouldn't  have come." We all burst out laughing at the guy.

author: @BanjoLuke1
text:
I'll never know, but I wonder sometimes whether Rains' character was so named because of Louis Renault and his company.
I'm not sure how widely Renault's passive (?) collaboration with the German occupiers was known in Hollywood at the time this film was written/made.
However... Captain Renault lives in Vichy Casablanca in the very same fog of deceit and pretence that Louis Renault used to profit from occupation. 
I may be wrong.
But I do wonder...

No depth, no glow.

text: Casablanca was released in 1942, which is well before the end of WWII.  It was a movie of the times, which makes it remarkable.  It is based on the play 'Everybody Comes to Rick's' written in 1940 by people who had firsthand knowledge of what was happening in the south of France. They sold the play to Warner Bros for $20,000.

author: @mariolucero8559
text: CAFÉ EL PERICO AZUL" Cuyo dueño Rick Blaine (Humphery Bogart) y su Pianista cantor Dooley Wilson cómo Sam) son los qué atienden en las noches a sus clientela con melodías  nacidas desde el piano. Pasado largo tiempo, regresa el amor con quién vivió momentos inolvidables (Ingrid   Bergman,cómo  Lisa Lund) Sam,tenía prohibido  tocar y cantar "A Medida  que Pasa el Tiempo" ;pues,era la canción de ellos cuando eran 1:37  felices.Lisa Lund,llega al café y lo primero qué pide es la canción de ella y Nick.Entre enojos y calma,ella presenta a su nuevo amor ( Paul Henreid ) un hombre importante,para "Resistencia Francesa el cual debe huir hacia otro destino.Rick,deja de lado sus vivencias junto a quién amó tanto;para ello,consigue un vuelo apoyado por Autoridades de  Justicia y huye de Casablanca.Moraleja: Más allá de creer Rick,qué volvería el reencuentro amoroso,se incluye en el escape del líder y queda "sólo " una vez más, la cual sería la última. " Tócala de nuevo Sam " es espectacular el  piano y la voz ronquesina de Sam.

author: @clemlockyer3868
text: Early in 1939 WW2 started, I lived in Gibraltar and was 8 years old, the Italians bombed Gibraltar twice and the the civilians were evacuated to Casablanca that was French at that time. The French capitulated and we returned to Gibraltar and then evacuated to Madeira. The experience in Casablanca was quite educational as a young girl and very bad traveling there and back in sort of cargo boats trying to avoid German  submarines and taking three days to travel to and from and sleeping on deck with the food we had been able to carry with us. It was quite an experience I will never forget.

author: @Boug0014
text: It’s quite interesting with those street guides.  Some (I’m not saying all), don’t even know the history …. The Portuguese never occupied Rabat…. They occupied Maamoura in 1515 about 30km north of Rabat and Casablanca in also 1515.  But they never were in Rabat.  The Oudaya Kasbah was built by the Almohad Royal Family in 1150 as a fortification (Ribat) to fight the Berghwata Kingdom that extended from south of Rabat to about Essaouira.  The Kasbah was later used as a settlement for the Andalus Refugees after the fall of Granada and the expulsion of the Moriscos from Spain.

author: @gon20hig
text: Chellah (Shalla) and Hassan Mosque are two major historical sites in Rabat closely linked to Amazigh history. Chellah was originally inhabited by local Amazigh populations .the site later became the Roman city of Sala Colonia in the 1st century AD. During the Amazigh Almohad period in the 12th century, Chellah regained importance as a sacred area, before being developed into a fortified necropolis under the Amazigh Marinid dynasty in the 13th–14th centuries. Hassan Mosque and its minaret (Hassan Tower) were commissioned by Yaqub al-Mansur, an Amazigh Almohad caliph, around 1191–1197 CE, as part of an ambitious project to make Rabat an imperial capital, but construction stopped after his death in 1199 CE, leaving the tower unfinished and symbolic of Almohad architectural power.

Hi Chris, compliments on your videos, I really enjoy them!
I’m a street photographer living in London and in March I’m going for a week in Marrakech. I’ve heard that sometimes people can be a bit hostile if they notice they’re being photographed, or may ask for money. I always try to be very respectful, quiet and discreet when I shoot, but I was wondering what your honest opinion is. If I’m travelling alone for a week, do you think a city like Rabat or Fez might be a better option for street photography, with people being a bit less confrontational? I’d really appreciate your thoughts. Thanks again for the great content.

text: Old graves by/on the beach is actually a very common thing around the world - sunbathing and spending time on the beach is only a thing of the modern era. In previous centuries people had no use for beaches, as they are not arable land, so instead of creating cemeteries on good fertile land inland they would put them along a beach, or on the beach in some instances, to make use of otherwise useless space.

text:
Morroco is a corrupt country. The law and constitution is broken. 

Law and order only exists if bribes are paid. 

The court system is rigged where you always fall short of their demands. 

I was a landlord in Marrakesh. I tried to evict the tenant, the case is still active even after 4 years.

I would urge anyone not to invest in Morroco as the people and system is corrupt and broken.

author: @DLPdiaries
text: Marrakech is so amazing! We stayed in a Riad in the Medina and did excursions to the Atlas mountains (blew our mind, so beautiful!), Essouira and a quad tour through the palmeraie. And of course wandered through the medina, had the best food and experiences in the gardens and djamaa El Fna etc. I would realy reccomend visiting. The Moroccans are SO welcoming and kind! You do need to keep in mind to haggle in the shops, and not fall for the monkey scams on the square or ' i'll show you the way' scams but other than that...such amazing people and such a beautiful culture!

author: @ArjunLSen
text: Marrakesh is boring, the people working in the tourism industry are patronising and unfriendly, and you can get really ill eating the food. It was a massive disappointment and the most overrated destination I've been to in recent years. I live in Spain and there's more historic buildings in your average Spanish town than in the city of Marrakesh. The whole place is a big sell and a big con.  Avoid Marrakesh and probably Morocco. Africa is a big place and there are a lot more interesting and better value places to visit.

author: @truthsetyoufree104
text: The destinations you've explored don't capture the full essence of Morocco. A significant portion of the population grapples quietly with challenges like unemployment, inadequate education and healthcare, poverty, and mental health struggles. Additionally, a number of individuals, driven by desperation, risk their lives attempting to migrate to Europe. This is the stark reality that some influencers may overlook or choose not to share, but global awareness is growing, and the broader truths are increasingly hard to conceal.

author: @bravo2966
text:
@CelticMickTravels Sounds good. I'm torn for 2026 whether to use Amsterdam as a base, hire a car and visit a lot of WW2 sites over Northern Europe or do Marakech.
As the airport for Amsterdam is a 10 minute drive from my house and the Marakech airport would be London, a 5-6 hour drive for me, I'm leaning towards Amsterdam atm. From same local airport to Marakech it's like £400 more than the London price and has a layover...so a bit of a bugger whichever way I do it.

text: i just got back from a vacation in Morocco (Marrakech and Essaouira), I cant stop thinking about it, I think Morocco might be my favorite place ive  traveled to so far. Not everywhere is the best food (think tourist traps) but if you read up on some reviews you can really find some of the most amazing foods! I'm sitting here at home missing it all! author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Yes, paying close attention to landmarks helps a lot, and we tried to use the same paths as often as we could. It's challenging when you want to explore, because you can go deep and find yourself in a maze where everything starts to look the same! Some people suggest having a walking tour to help you navigate, but we seemed to figure it out okay on our own. By the time we did a walking tour, we had become pretty comfortable navigating it on our own. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Donna, Knees and shoulders is more of a Western modesty ideal when you're heading into a church. Some Muslim women are covered head to toe. But at the same time, Marrakech and Fes and Chefchaouen are pretty tolerant of Westerners. We noticed a wide range of dress among locals and visitors alike. You don't need to wear headscarves anywhere, especially since you're not allowed in a mosque if you're not a Muslim (in Morocco). I felt my clothes were reasonably modest while also being comfortable for the heat. As long as you're not baring your midriff, showing off too much cleavage, or showing off your thighs, I think you're good. Bear in mind that some Moroccans wear more clothes because they're also trying not to tan too much/protect their skin versus exclusively for modesty purposes. We hope you have a terrific time in Morocco! —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi Muhammed, We LOVE London. In fact our daughter was just there two days ago after visiting with us in Malta! May will be a nice time to visit Marrakech. The weather is hot but not unbearable, especially if you avoid the hottest parts of the day. And May is a month of celebrations. The Kelaa M'Gouna or Festival of the Rose runs from May 5-9 if you will be around to attend that. Have a terrific time! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi Laura, The culture in Morocco is more modest, so if you want to respect the local culture, you won't wear clothes that are too revealing. I'm wearing tank tops, and my skirts are close to knee length, but you could cover your arms with an airy blouse and wear loose long pants or long skirts. I wasn't going to go out and buy these items since we're full time travelers, but I tried to be as modest as possible given my limited wardrobe. Morocco is reasonably understanding of tourists and don't necessarily expect them to follow all the Muslim customs. Personally, I wouldn't wear crop tops or show off my stomach or my full back or wear a bathing suit top (although we saw all of these by tourists). Does this help at all? —Judy

text:
Hi Angela, There are no required vaccinations that the Moroccan government checks to see if you have had. However, with the monkeys in the Sahara, it wouldn't be the worst thing to have a rabies vaccination (we do not have that vaccination). We made sure that we had Hepatitis A, B, tetanus, MMR (measles, mumps, and rubella), typhoid, and cholera before we traveled to Egypt. If you have any specific concerns, it's not a bad idea to check with a travel doctor who can give you recommendations. But specific to Morocco? Nothing is required for entry. —Judy

author: @tkozikow
text:
​@Kaedehara_egiap we booked our excursions directly through our riad, and the highlight was a day trip to the Ourika Valley. We had a private driver who arranged for a guide in Setti Fadma to accompany us on a waterfall hike.   There are plenty of small bus/group tours, but I would highly recommend spending the extra money... It was around $175, including the tip, for two people. We stopped at a couple of Berber villages along the way and purchased some pottery and a rug for a fraction of the cost it would be in Marrakech. The guide was around $40 and well worth the money. If you decide to do this, be sure to have decent shoes that are OK for light hiking.

I suggest hiring a guide to explore the souks and key areas inside the medina. You might encounter some sales pitches along the way, but it was an interesting five hours and provided us with ideas for what to explore on our own. You can easily book either of these through a service like Viator, but if you're staying at a decent riad, they should be able to coordinate this for you.

Definitely explore the alleyways and small streets in the medina for the best food and a glimpse of the culture. Marakech is very tourist-friendly, with something like 80% of its revenue based on this, and we never felt unsafe.

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi @rosier3188, I want to be clear that I understand your question. I did book tours when I was in Morocco (but we are full-time travelers and that's the only time that worked for us—BUT we were there 40 days and weren't in a time crunch). We recommend ONLY ever booking your excursions online for the best experience. If you wait until you show up in Morocco and go to one of the vendors shops, you might be able to get a discount, BUT you will have a much lesser experience. We talk about it in our Sahara Desert video, and although the point we make is specifically for that type of excursion, it really applies to any type of excursion. On the street, they don't care what kind of experience you give them because there's a higher likelihood that (1) you won't leave a review; (2) people who book from those vendors don't typically even look at reviews; (3) they know that they are appealing to broad masses, so the entire experience tends to be more plain than what you book in advance. That's also because the vendors know that their reputation is on the line, which isn't necessarily the case for street vendors with shops. Note that it's illegal for them to work out the details with you on the street, so they need to hustle you into their kiosk to sort out the details.

If you are referring to our Sahara Desert tour, we would suggest you book it early online and talk with the vendor in advance so that you get exactly what you want. Here is our full series, which includes tons of details about our Sahara Desert tour and the vendor we used (not affiliated in any way and make no money from recommending them). If you need me to explain it better, please let me know! —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

author: @jmw-qt2ih
@FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi again, @jmw-qt2ih! The food on the Sahara Desert tour will be the same food you get everywhere else in Morocco. Tagine will be everywhere and so will couscous. Couscous is its own meal served vegetarian with vegetables (large pieces of a few assorted vegetables like carrot, butternut squash, potato and chickpeas) or also including a choice of chicken, beef, or lamb. Tagine is basically meat with a few vegetables on top (butternut squash, carrots, and preserved lemons), which also can be vegetarian. Kevin ate a lot of kefta, which is basically beef meatballs in a tomato sauce. On the tour we also got a tomato/cucumber salad in most places. They often also had fruit as dessert, and bread was always served at meals. Our videos from Morocco show a fairly good representation of the food we ate throughout the 41 days we were in Morocco. Breakfasts would typically include yogurt, fresh fruit juice, tea, and sometimes pastries. 

Just to be a little clearer, we ate a breakfast the first day just as we were starting off at a coffee shop/grocery store for tourists and lunch was also at a local restaurant (the cost was not included in the excursion price). Dinner and breakfast the second morning was at our accommodations and included soup and was a bit fancier. Day 2 lunch was at a local restaurant (again you ordered off the menu and the cost was not included in the tour). Dinner was served family style in our desert accommodations and was very nice (chicken tagine with soup and fruit for dessert and bread). Breakfast at our desert accommodations included pastries, bread, yogurt. Lunch was at a restaurant and we again chose from a menu. 

Let me know if you have any other questions. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Dan, We understand that a lot of this is very cultural. I learned the hard way when I tried to take a photo of a butcher stall and the shop worker gave me the finger (and probably a few curse words). Some people in Morocco believe in the evil eye and are actually afraid of cameras. Some Muslims also have issues with "graven images" of themselves and having their picture taken is against their religious beliefs. Other people are just worried about someone copying their handiwork. Not everyone in Morocco feels this way, but we try to be respectful of those who do. 

But you are probably referring to places like the Yves Saint Laurent museum and Berber museum. We hate that, too. There were restrictions in certain places in Albania as well, and some places will allow you to take photos but not video. It's hard for us to go places that we can't share with our viewers, and we agree some of it feels petty. We've never encountered anything like Madrid where we were watched like hawks in a few of the museums there. And even when we weren't on YouTube, we still liked having photos as memories of our experiences, so thanks for relating! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi @dotted8- For some people in Morocco there's a fear of the evil eye, for others there's a fear of having their unique ideas stolen, and others have conservative religious beliefs that make them uncomfortable with photos. We try our best to respect the local customs wherever we visit, but you are entitled not to patronize a place that doesn't allow photos. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: I now am realizing that you were referring to the Berber museum and the Yves Saint Laurent museum. Yeah, not taking photos was supposed to keep the art there feeling "special," and perhaps "exclusive," but it's a huge bummer for us when photos are restricted. Sorry I didn't understand this better in my original response! I was mentally more focused on all the souks and people who didn't want their pictures taken. I'd almost forgotten about the issue with the museums (out of sight; out of mind—just one more reason that they SHOULD allow photos—so you can look back on those memories later!) —Judy

It was such an incredible experience. We highly recommend you see Fes if you can as well (and do a Sahara Desert experience if you can!). Fes and Marrakech are vastly different in many ways, but at their core, there are wonderfully kind people in both. Let us know if you have any questions we should incorporate into any of our Morocco series before the final two episodes are released! —Judy
🎥 MOROCCO: 22 DO’s and DON’Ts for Marrakech, Fes, and the Sahara Desert ❤️ https://youtu.be/ZPENQNxbBhU

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi Marcus, thanks for watching and thank you for your comment! How amazing of a trip you will have with your father! We're glad to hear that this episode was helpful. We had so much good food while we were there, but we did hope it would help by mentioning everything we saw and ate. We can't wait to bring you with us to our experience in the Sahara Desert, which was a bucket list item for sure. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Thank you so much for watching and for letting us know about the Majorelle/YSL Museum combo you took. We'd heard good things about the YSL Museum, but since we couldn't take photos and share them with our viewers, it had slightly less appeal for us. And we heard mixed reviews about Jardin Majorelle because it had become a bit tired looking, but we can't say if that was actually the case. I didn't have much of the kefta until we got to Fes, where I ate significantly more of it, but Kevin's were always good. Fes had a bit more spice at times, which was a happy treat, so there were differences in flavors between cities. We considered seeing more of the country than we did, but ultimately were glad we were able to be immersed in Marrakech and Fes for longer. We did a day trip to Meknes and Volubilis as a day trip from Fes as well, that we'll be sharing in two weeks. Kevin changed it up with a few burgers (including camel burgers) and also pizza a couple of times in Fes. One nice thing about Fes was that our Riad's breakfast was really delicious and satisfying, so we only needed to go out for one meal per day, which slowed some of the tagine burnout for us! —Judy

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: I said in the video that I was overwhelmed with the noise, crowd and fellow tourists. I have a love-hate relationship with the market. And you have no right to say that we are dishonest in our video. We go out as early as possible to beat the crowd,  and heat and it is always quieter in the morning. It is your fault for going out late then complain if it’s crowded. You should know the culture of the places that you visit. We also gave tips in the video, which you would know, IF you watched and listened to be careful of scammers. We knew about that by watching other travel videos and reading blogs before going. Again, it was your fault for not preparing yourself mentally of all the possibilities. Tourists are there to learn their culture, not to change it. If you did not like your experience, you have no right to accuse us of being dishonest when we clearly mentioned everything. Next time, before you talk, you listen to everything we say and have better understanding. If you do not like the crowd, go in the mountains!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
It's Chano here answering your comment, I am from Spain and I grew up in a Christian family, in case you may think I am Muslim because I love travelling to Morocco and learning about their history and culture. 
There is not historic evidence about a church being located where the Koutoubia Mosque was built. But our Cathedral of Sevilla was built on top of a previous mosque, and the old minaret was transformed into the beautiful Giralda bell tower, which is considered the architectural sister of the Koutoubia Minaret. 
The Spanish Christians built many churches on top of mosques after the Reconquista of Al-Andalus, and the Arabs did the same before as well, building mosques on top of Roman temples and Visigothic churches. And the Romans did the same on top of other religions temples. It was very common to repurpose materials or sites from previous religious buildings.
If you try to spread misinformation in our content we will have to block you. We respect different religions and ideas, so please do not spam our comment section with hatred. Thanks.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Nassa.
Thanks a million for watching our video and for appreciating our work! 🙏🏽 We're very happy to read your positive feedback and very kind words. Comments like yours keep us motivated to continue working hard and to share more travel documentaries around the world in the future. 🎥💪🏽😊

Regarding the herbal infusion, we cannot remember the price, but it wasn't expensive at all, they said it was done with mint, but not sure if something else was included. We still have some crystals at home, we use them with very little quantity and put boiling water when we have very bad hay fever for example, it helps to open the nose and it feels refreshing in the throat too. We haven't drunk it anymore though, as it was too strong, and just inhaling the boiling water it is more than enough for us. 
The name they had on their sign is: Herboriste Des Amis, and they had the following phone number on the sign too: 024382076 (Morocco's international prefix: +212). Google Maps: https://share.google/KlQ2z5FO6dCXCQQfl
We advise you contact your doctor if you have some health issues before taking traditional medicines too. Hope that helps!

We wish you all the very best to you too, have a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️😊 Let us know if you have other questions in other videos, we are always happy to share information about travelling with our viewers. Best regards!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Did we claim that we know the city? author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Thanks for watching. I think we have showed in the video that most local people were very friendly to us. Of course, like in all the big cities in the world, you need to have common sense. We have explained in the video about how to behave in the souks to avoid issues. Take the usual safety measures like the ones you would take visiting big cities like Rome, Paris or Madrid, just to mention some big cities we have visited in recent years, where you have to keep your belongings safe in crowded places. Do not believe scammers approaching you in the middle of the street. Do not stay alone late at night in areas where there are not many people. In our whole trip in Morocco we felt safe, you can watch the rest of our travel series to have a broader idea. We caught public buses (even at night), the train, we stayed after sunset and woke up for sunrise. Not a single issue. We met some wonderful people who just wanted to chat on the streets too. If someone suddenly offers you to bring you somewhere or to sell you something, in any country in the world, just don't trust them. If they are just curious and friendly, be open to a nice conversation. 🙂

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Thank you for watching our video!

We didn´t exchange money in Morocco, we have a Revolut card that allow us to withdraw money for free in other countries up to 200€ per month, and then after that we pay only 1€ commission for every withdrawal. But you have to find the proper ATMs or Banks in every country to avoid paying commissions there. In Morocco, we only used the Al Barid Bank ATMs and we never paid commission with them. I would advise you to find them in Google Maps in every city in advance to save their location, there are not that many and better be prepared with enough cash, but if you are only visiting Marrakech, you have an Al Barid Bank ATM at the airport in the departures area (walk to the right side immediately before exiting out the airport). Then you have 2 or 3 more Al Barid Bank ATMs in the Jemma el-Fnaa square just outside the Post Office building. Sometimes we could pay in Restaurants and Hotels directly with our Revolut debit card. They always have great currency exchange.

We often show dishes with meat as you can see in our video, but we remember that in almost every restaurant that we show in this video, they had vegetarian dishes as well, you can find easily falafel, vegetarian couscous or vegetarian tajine. The tacos can be found without meat as well and they often have soups and salads done with only vegetables. So I think Morocco is a good country for a vegetarian to try local cuisine as well. And don´t forget their desserts! ☺️

Hope our reply was useful. Wish you a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Kitiwan, 
Thank you very much for watching our Morocco Travel Series and for appreciating our work. We´re really glad to know that the videos have been useful for preparing your coming trip to Morocco. You will love it there for sure! 🇲🇦❤️😊
So, ladies can wear whatever they want in public, in fact, sometimes we saw some local young women showing their legs with modern outfits, and many tourists were wearing short dresses as well. But at the same time, Morocco is a muslim country and they have certain cultural traditions and I preferred to respect that during our visit. I had heard some women receiving bad comments for dressing too sexy while visiting Morocco, but I only received smiles & nice comments about my dresses and the locals were often happy to see me with certain clothes. My husband helped me to choose some dresses before we visited, because he had already been there and he also knew the best colours for matching with the architecture 😅
In December, it may be cold early in the morning and at night too, so be careful with that, specially in the north of the country and in the desert. The Moroccan sun is very strong during the day, so traditional long clothing is actually the best for the desert for example. 
Hope my answer helped you a little bit!
All the best and wish you have a wonderful trip there!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Hello there Tom! There are indeed scams but for us they are very obvious. Also it was a great help that we watched a lot of videos before going there so we already knew what to avoid. For example, a random man who says that the way is closed then he will guide us to the correct way. Then he will ask money when you will arrive to the place. Do not believe them and just say no thank you. A lot of the merchants will be very friendly to you too and ask where are you from to start a conversation. We just answered them politely but we kept walking to where we were headed. If you know are lost for example in the souks because it’s like a labyrinth, just ask a merchant and probably avoid asking a random guy in the street. We were fine. We avoided “rough” areas. We used common sense. They are not going to hurt you. Some of them are just trying to earn money. Just ignore or say thank you. We felt safe in general. A lot of them are very friendly too. We met a family who just wanted to speak and practice their English. We are still in contact with them and invited us to visit their town.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
About the second question, Sardinia and Morocco are totally different. It depends on what you want to experience.

Sardinia is for beautiful Mediterranean coasts, coastal towns, chill life, amazing well kept nature, friendly locals, good food.

Morocco if you want to experience North African culture, more lively chaotic but unique culture, good food, mix of African and European architecture. And by the way, Moroccan architecture is impeccable and incomparable. They have one of the best designs I’ve ever seen. As a matter of fact, I want a touch of Moroccan design in my future house. Haha!! 

So if you decide which ones you want to experience, choose one. If you have more questions, feel free to ask us. We have Sardinia videos too.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Also, be careful of other videos describing the scams in Morocco. We found them too exaggerated. We’re not saying that scamming doesn’t exist but sometimes they were too much. And I think it is becoming more and more famous through social media so they are more careful now too.

text: I went to Tangier around 2004...and I didn't feel like it was "dangerous" at all. It definitely looks like it has changed though by looking at your video. More modern looking. It was like that in Spain too, like not that many tourists, and now I hear there's people everywhere.. And long lines into the attractions wow. I'm going back for the second time, next spring. By the way... Is there soap, water, and tissue in the bathrooms? Do the bathrooms even work, of you're able to find one? That's how it awash when we went around 2003-2004. Yess!!! I remember the fish market there!!

author: @SueHutton
text: I see this was made in 2010. Many big changes in Tangier road systems since then and there is a project to 'join up' Tangier and Malabata bays. The view will be amazing as cruise liners come into port. If anyone is up, that is. They tend to come in before dawn. The medina is just the same. Oh, and the clubs and bars along the corniche have all been demolished for a vision of a 'clean' Tangier which is family friendly. That area will also look on to a marina. The fishing port is being shunted sideways. They're supposed to be trying to keep the walls of the Kasbah but they look very precarious.

author: @duffgordon9005
text: Tangier was THE PICK of ATCO RECORDS (CREME) for that great label (on hiatus) to be 'reborn' . They hired Andy Johns as Producer - did the MTV films.. put a lot of $$ into them.and promotion. They toured with Cinderalla , 38 Special-- If you go to Amazon and put in their LP's , you will read, from HARD CORE ROCKERS.. 5 star after 5 star, and even Anger- "how come I never knew about these guys"!!  The problem imo was 3-fold. 1) It was the end of the glammish rockers, and the opposite came flooding in- KURT COBAIN and the like 2)  The key to the staying power is "a HIT"  if not number having a number one- All their soings are "hittable" but it just did not happen. I always say, play ALL three LP's and chances are you will not sigh and "skip songs here and there. " Doug Gordon, started playing guitar at 16, and got signed at 28- supplying dozens of songs, and was featured in Guitar PLayer. He always said, I am either going to make it, and continue on, or "that's that" It's a testament that their music has not aged.I love the Remastering of 4-winds, . my suprise is not a single Movie, Commercial ever got picked out say "FEVER for GOLD " on one of these many mining shows..

author: @rotadymaify
text: @nathanlansford1882 80’s rock were the masters of musicianship. Big melody vocals and hooks, screaming guitar solos, big production and actually putting on a show for their fans. Great stage presence. Not like the bull shit that poisoned the music scene in the 90’s. Flannel shirts, greasy hair, singing about suicide, no melody to the music and no guitar solos. No wonder grunge only lasted a few years. It sucked.

author: @nathanlansford1882
text:
Plenty of people wanted it though, by that point. They were sick of hundreds of pop-glam metal bands doing the same types of songs with the same types of lyrics all the time. 

From 1988 to '91, that was increasingly the case. And it wasn't only the grunge fans that felt that way about it--so did the underground speed/thrash, death/grindcore & black metal fans (& bands).

author: @rotadymaify
text: @nathanlansford1882 some of what you say is true but there were still tens of millions that didn’t want it to end. I have never stopped listening to that genre and have a catalogue of over 2000 CD’s of that genre. Of course I like a lot of styles of music but Hair metal or hard rock from late 70’s to early 90’s is a part of a lot of peoples best times of their lives. There was never a better time to be alive.

text: I remember visiting the historic section of Galveston with my mom on a bus tour of Texas.  We were let out at the visitor's center and informed that they were filming a movie in the historic area.  We could go in anyway, if we stayed out of the cameras' way.  Mom and I said, let's go.  We found them filming exterior shots along one street, and located a hamburger stand behind the camera set up.  We had lunch watching them film antique cars and a tram over and over,  talked to a few extras in costume, and took pictures of the cameras and a wagon full of palm trees.  It was great.  When we got back to the visitor's center, we discovered no one else had left the building, scared off by the idea of the camera crew.  A vacation is what you make of it.

text:
Las Ramblas, Barcelona lol in 2007 we were strolling down the sidewalk in the evening (with a 8 year old and a 15 year old) and my husband and I looked up to see a few transvestites. We immediately turned around.

But I found the subway to be more of a pick pocket hazard. I had my 8 year old by the hand as we stepped on. Immediately 2 men stepped between us. I got loud and didnt let go of my son. I also had my money in a pouch under my clothing. The two men immediately got off.

The worst pick pocket experience I had tho was in Rome near the Colosseum. We decided to take a short cut on our way back to our hotel. I was immediately mobbed by a bunch of kids one of whom had a square of cardboard which she pushed up against my chest. The idea is that while she got my attention, one of the other kids would be underneath picking my pocket. Too bad for them my money was in a pouch they didnt have access too.

I’ve been to visit the Pyramids. It was a 4 day stop as a part of our honeymoon. Luckily we had booked through travel agency so we had guides. We werent bothered by people trying to seperate us from our money. The Pyramids are a site to see.

Trevi Fountain is very crowded (thus the high rate of pick pockets). It’s very beautiful.

I’ve been to many of the places on the list. I usually enjoy myself. What it comes down to is you need to do your research and know what you are in for. None of this is really hidden. You just need to research and read reviews.

author: @johncraven9719
text:
The subject seems a non-starter.

One might think that living or realising a dream would always give pleasure to someone. But buying a product without really asking the right questions is almost bound to lead to worries, anger, disappointment.
BTW one of life's principles is "disappointment is the inevitable result of expecting too much". Many would book a cheap hôtel in order to increase their holiday spending budget or because of their youthful enthusiasm, but would be stupid to complain about the lack of comfort or quiet!
Those who travel while constantly comparing things to "how it was back home" are also bound to become narrow-minded and dissatisfied. To voyage with a sense of curiosity and discovery is a better mindset.

Then again do you want sandy beaches, mountain lakes, hiking, total relaxing, a hired car, being part of a tourist guided group, alone, with someone special? Have you got travel within the country sorted out? Do you know what currency is used (e.g. not every member country of the European Union uses the Euro as currency!)

author: @missusd1130
text: To avoid in Wisconsin: My home state: Events: The WI state fair, for much of the reasons stated here, also drinking events in Madison, for CRIME reasons, including being given knockout and rape drugs, esp. during Freak fest and the Mifflin street block party, and The Hodag Festival which turns out to be mostly a hidden Stugris drug, drunk, prostitute and hidden theft fest on hidden steroids.  If you want family friendly, county fairs are the way to go.  If you need something "more exciting", I suggest the Dells preferably out of the most active time--less expsneisve, less crowded, and way easier to schedule in the "off season" between mid August-mid June.  Yeah, avoid July visits.  Milwaukee should be avoided entirely unless you want to see things like the Betty Brin and other museums outside of crowded times.  Stay out of Milwaukee at night!  Don't park except in paid places, You car MIGHT be watched in those areas.  Most other places in WI are great including Door County, but look for a mosquito or tick report.  You'll thank me.  Biting flies can get guaranteed when those are active and some don't give a rip about DEET.  We've had a first hand experience of DEET failing to even affect ticks and biting flies, mine included a cases of Babiosis.  My recommendation, Visit in August through November to take advantage of the lovely foliage and harvest fest which can be quite family friendly and still have the brewskis you're wanting.  Easily Spring in great too, the bugs are still dead and the worst thing you'll experience is mud.  The camping is best in May and early June but in some places the ticks are out already.  August if ideal drying out and the sicks start to die out.  In SD where I live now stay away from Crazy Horse Monument.  It's not done and insanely expensive.  THey're right about Mt Rushmore, the parking is insanely expensive and don't visit on a cloudy day!  Buy a start park pass the SD state parks are all great, not just Custer and really worth visiting and has less ticks and mosquitoes than WI except during July  Outside of July and January/February and with a careful following of weather reports (because in May we get summer one day and winter the next LOL!).  If you're a cheapie, Spearfish anyone is FREE to explore!  And the city of Spearfish is a tourist paradise with not too far Rapids City having a lot of great sights, places to visit and even hiking areas that are great views and not hard to manage.

author: @durasaxon5131
text:
I believe I've been to 4 or 5 of these tourist traps that are mentioned herein.
Yes, I've been to the Vatican in Rome and as well St Paul's Cathedral in London. 
The #1 not a tourist trap that I've walked through is 
The Little Bighorn in Montana 
This is where the 7th Cavalry met its defeat.
George Armstrong Custer. 
I was the only person who was walking through the fields of the Little Big Horn
on that day in October 1997.
It wasn't a tourist trap in 1997 
nor was hiking along the Lewis and Clark Trail. 
I have no idea what has become of such an experience but somebody may let me know here in the 
Comments. 

Finally, hiking and experiencing the USA in 1997 was an incredible experience but we know that commercialization probably has taken over since then? 
I've always remembered to take in the Churches of the States and locations that I visited whether in the USA or elsewhere.

author: @rickymcc8624
text:
For sure just about anywhere can feel like "hell on Earth" if you choose to go unprepared, in high season, without doing proper research first and managing your expectations.

I have seen and enjoyed many of these, but season and time of day matters hugely.

IMO it's best to avoid anywhere when a massive cruise ship docks and a sunami of tourists wash inland.

So my top tips.
1) Do lots of research before travelling. Read Trip advisor reviews and if possible advice from friends who might have already been to place X.

2) Set realistic expectations, based on your research.

3) Ideally travel in the 'quieter' season. Get up and out at first light 5, 6 or 7am to have a real chance of missing the crowds.

author: @mtnsidhe
text:
Having been to a number of these places, I don't agree with all of these assessments. For instance, travelers should always expect and protect themselves from pickpockets, especially in Europe.

Pisa should be a 2-hour stop between other places, and do climb to the top if you can. Avoid the toilets in the restaurants, they are literally a white-tiled hole in the floor.

Venice is just awful. Not only crowded, over priced, touristy and smelly, but also hot, muggy, and mosquito-infested. There are no screens on the windows and no AC, so sleeping at night requires open windows, and fat, blood-filled mosquitos in your room come morning. If you do go, be sure to see St. Mark's Square and Cathedral and feed the pigeons for a photo.


Versailles should be on this list. Long lines, huge entrance fee, shuffling through one enormous cube-shaped room after another, all filled with gilt-framed paintings and ostentatious rococco furniture and priceless gewgaws, all behind ropes, over and over and over until they all blur together. Lots of walking and standing in return for feeling simultanelously overwhelmed and underwhelmed, plus tired and bored. There is a separate tour for the gardens, which may have been better, but still, probably lots of walking, only in the sun.

author: @Andalus4Ever
text:
Let’s look at it from a regulatory standpoint, because several key concepts are being misunderstood.

First: the argument that “the referee’s decision is final” is being misapplied.

Yes, under Law 5, the referee’s decisions during the match are final in terms of on-field play.

But that does not mean the result is immune from post-match disciplinary review.

That’s a completely separate layer of governance.

Now, the core issue here is straightforward: a team leaving the field of play without the referee’s permission constitutes a regulatory breach.

And the rules on that are not ambiguous:

- Leaving the field without authorization is considered a serious violation

- It can lead to a forfeit, regardless of what happens afterward

So the argument that “they didn’t leave the stadium, only the pitch” is irrelevant.

The obligation is to participate in the match on the field of play, not merely to remain somewhere inside the venue.

Now, here’s the key misunderstanding:

“The game restarted, so everything before that is erased.”

That is simply incorrect.

Football governance clearly distinguishes between:

- The sporting outcome on the field, and

- The regulatory outcome after review

A referee allowing play to continue does not nullify a prior breach of the rules.

Disciplinary bodies exist precisely to address situations where infractions occurred, regardless of how the match was managed in real time.

Let me put it in a simple analogy:

Imagine a marathon runner who takes a shortcut off the official route, then re-enters the race and crosses the finish line first. At the moment, officials might not stop them. The race “finishes.”

But once the violation is reviewed, the result is corrected — because the rules of the competition were broken.

The same principle applies here.

Finally, claims of “disgrace,” “illegality,” or “backroom decisions” are speculative and not substantiated by the regulatory framework. These decisions are made within established procedures, and they remain subject to appeal — including at CAS.

So the bottom line is this:

Emotions about fairness are understandable.
But they do not override the rules.

And the rules are clear:
if a team leaves the field of play without authorization, it exposes itself to disciplinary sanctions — including forfeiture — even if the match was later resumed.

That’s not opinion.
That’s the regulatory framework.

Dima Maghrib ❤️🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦🇲🇦❤

author: @LahlouAbde-f7j3n
text: The Senegalese national team displayed a blatant lack of respect towards the FIFA president, the CAF president, the tournament organizing committee, and the senior sports officials present at the stadium during the final match between Senegal and Morocco, the host country of the 2025 CAF World Cup in the Kingdom of Morocco. The players left the pitch for the locker room for approximately fifteen minutes. They may have showered, had a drink, or used their phones before returning to finish the match with utter insolence and disrespect. Their entry and exit from the pitch mid-match, without any legal basis, freely and without sanction, constitute the greatest disgrace in the history of world football. Furthermore, they provoked unrest and clashes between Senegalese supporters and Moroccan security forces, tarnishing what was arguably the best and most impressive tournament ever held in Africa. However, these actions were orchestrated and instigated by parties hostile to Morocco in order to sabotage Moroccan and continental celebrations. Therefore, severe sanctions are necessary, and the law must be enforced. This should serve as a lesson for the future of African and world football.

They walked off the pitch for 15 damn minutes, crying like victims, while two Moroccan security agents were killed and journalists had their equipment destroyed. Yet somehow, when Moroccan players are forced to deal with chaos and provocation, they’re painted as the villains.

This narrative is irrational and disgusting. From the first whistle, the world rushed to judge Morocco instead of looking at the facts. Senegal has already pulled similar scenes against Egypt and Tunisia — and now they do it again in an AFCON final, on the biggest stage, knowing exactly how the media will spin it.

Morocco didn’t create the disorder — they endured it. But once again, Moroccans are expected to stay silent while others play victim and rewrite reality. Enough. Truth matters, and Morocco deserves fairness, not scapegoating.

I’ve been posting high quality content every day on my Instagram, sometimes daily but I have over 230 posts now and I barely get 16 views on carousels and 200-600 views on reels. Nobody comments or likes, stories I only ever get the same 6-12 people. I’ve been stuck like this for a year now.

Filming all my content with an FX30 and super specific. It’s an old account that I revived, the only people who follow me are onlyfans accounts with links or content growth creators following me to just dm me that they can help me grow my account.

What the hell am I doing wrong?

author: @AntoinetteSarpong
text: Congrats! How often were you posting each day?

author: @Ohnoplata12
text:
​@AntoinetteSarpongmy rule for myself is don't go more than 2 days without a reel/post. Could I potentially grow more if I was posting multiple times a day, making multiple stories, going live, commenting on similar posts to mine as soon as I make a new post, and everything else all the gurus say? Yes probably. 

But, the reality Is that when I was doing that? I enjoyed it for all of the week or two and then it just turned me off or I'd become uninterested. Now I do three to five posts a week, I'm not worrying about thumbnails and cool editing. I'm just focusing on consistency. And I'm happy how it's slowly and authentically growing.

author: @AntoinetteSarpong
text: @Ohnoplata12 yeah, that makes sense! I’m a small creator as well and I’m finding the same cadence is what I can do consistently.

I ask you by Allah, the Living, the Eternal, to help me. By Allah, the Almighty, even at night we cannot sleep from fear. We do not have mattresses or blankets to warm ourselves from the cold. Everything is flooded from the rain, and we have no one but Allah and then you.  My brothers, I kiss your boots, don’t turn me away empty-handed. Help us with whatever you can. Is it acceptable to you that we live in this place? We are girls and we have no one. Our father died in a car accident. Consider us your daughters and your honor, and help me with whatever you can. author: @Salim2731-q2n
text:
C’est une sottise de croire que seuls la confession, ou ceux qui portent la nationalité algérienne sont les Algériens. 
Algérien, est un concept, une idée qu’on s’imprime. Une idée de résistance, de résilience, d’indépendance et de souveraineté ; l’algérien n’accepte jamais l’épée d’avilissement, l’oppression, l’anhélation, et hurlera toujours liberté. C’est celui qui éprouve de la joie quand il voit ses frères et sœurs en liberté, qu’il soit Palestinien-Gazaoui, Kanak ou Sahraoui, ainsi que les bravent Marocains qui refusent d’être locataire sur leurs terres ; quelles qu’en soient les circonstances et les difficultés. L’algérien, c’est celui qui partage avec ses frères et sœurs cette idée, et qu’importent leurs nationalités. 
L’Algérie ce n’est pas un pays qu’en habitent, mais une idée de liberté à laquelle en adhèrent.
Frères, sœurs de tout continent de tout pays, unissons-nous et déclarant notre Algérianité, en hurlons liberté !
Adhérer et partagez si vous êtes convaincu cela est de votre devoir.

author: @Salim2731-q2n
text:
C’est une sottise de croire que seuls la confession, ou ceux qui portent la nationalité algérienne sont les Algériens. 
Algérien, est un concept, une idée qu’on s’imprime. Une idée de résistance, de résilience, d’indépendance et de souveraineté ; l’algérien n’accepte jamais l’épée d’avilissement, l’oppression, l’anhélation, et hurlera toujours liberté. C’est celui qui éprouve de la joie quand il voit ses frères et sœurs en liberté, qu’il soit Palestinien-Gazaoui, Kanak ou Sahraoui, ainsi que les bravent Marocains qui refusent d’être locataire sur leurs terres ; quelles qu’en soient les circonstances et les difficultés. L’algérien, c’est celui qui partage avec ses frères et sœurs cette idée, et qu’importent leurs nationalités. 
L’Algérie ce n’est pas un pays qu’en habitent, mais une idée de liberté à laquelle en adhèrent.
Frères, sœurs de tout continent de tout pays, unissons-nous et déclarant notre Algérianité, en hurlons liberté !
Adhérer et partagez si vous êtes convaincu cela est de votre devoir.

author: @Salim2731-q2n
text:
C’est une sottise de croire que seuls la confession, ou ceux qui portent la nationalité algérienne sont les Algériens. 
Algérien, est un concept, une idée qu’on s’imprime. Une idée de résistance, de résilience, d’indépendance et de souveraineté ; l’algérien n’accepte jamais l’épée d’avilissement, l’oppression, l’anhélation, et hurlera toujours liberté. C’est celui qui éprouve de la joie quand il voit ses frères et sœurs en liberté, qu’il soit Palestinien-Gazaoui, Kanak ou Sahraoui, ainsi que les bravent Marocains qui refusent d’être locataire sur leurs terres ; quelles qu’en soient les circonstances et les difficultés. L’algérien, c’est celui qui partage avec ses frères et sœurs cette idée, et qu’importent leurs nationalités. 
L’Algérie ce n’est pas un pays qu’en habitent, mais une idée de liberté à laquelle en adhèrent.
Frères, sœurs de tout continent de tout pays, unissons-nous et déclarant notre Algérianité, en hurlons liberté !
Adhérer et partagez si vous êtes convaincu cela est de votre devoir.

author: @user-oc8jp2bk2y
text:
I am not into this music genre, but why does this art feels like the essence of my childhood?

Huh, that was pretty intense
I barely listened to music, I just paused, started at the art, felt something and wrote that comment, and now I am editing it telling that apprently it helped me
This does convey the vibe of my childhood, and I had to reprocess it for a bit
My childhood mostly was not happy, and mostly, traumatic shit happened to happen at summers, so I spent most either gaming or walking with friends outside (most of them were not doing okay too)
So... I recalled some memories and had to cry for the first time about it, I would probably get to that later otherwise but it happened here

author: @NickBurman
text:
The problem is that most MAGAts live in places which hardly have any tourists (Florida or Vegas excepted), let alone foreign tourists. And if one showed up, it would likely be a case of "MARTHA, GRAB DA SHOTGUN – THERE'S A DAMN FOREIGNER HANGIN' AROUND!" They don't have the faintest idea on how much tourism adds to the US economy and how their policies and choice(s) have damaged not only the economy but also the USA's reputation. 

And it's not from now either... there was a case which did the rounds of social media a long time ago of some redneck geezer in a US airport who picked on two Brazilians waiting for their flight home because they were speaking Portuguese among themselves. And another Brazilian, who had paused for lunch in a roadside diner during a road trip who was told  by a policemen to finish his meal and leave ASAP, or he would be arrested – for no reason whatsoever.

author: @Dom-cq8pw
text:
I am from Europe and I travelled to the US yearly for the last 13 years (failing some years because of covid restrictions ofc) on average for a 14 day long vacation
 I love the landscape and national parks and there is a lot I still want to see and explore

This year is the first time in a decade I am not travelling to the US willingly because I am genuinely worried that for some reason I could be denied entry for whatever reason - which obviously would not make any sense like I have been here 10+ times and I left every time never overstaying my visit or anything but I am genuinely worried that it could happen. That fear alone is enough to deter me besides the fact that I am also worried about what is going to happen tomorrow with this unhinged administration.

I am genuinely sad the US is an amazing country with so much to offer but well it is time to explore other parts of the worlds now I guess. The issue I see with this is also people me included might not return to the US as soon as the administration changes while protesting the US and travelling somewhere else they might fall in love with a different place/ different country / different continent and continue spending their money elsewhere even if the US returns back to how it used to be.

author: @Radtechgirl
text:
For a country that talks about capitalism so much you would expect them to understand when you insult and threaten your customers they aren’t your customers any longer.

Thank you for pointing out that the worst insult has been calling us the “51st state”.  Your country threatens our sovereignty with this deep disrespect and are then shocked when we don’t spend our money in your country.  I have seen so many reports that people in the US think we aren’t coming because of your weather haven’t seen how many of us will go to Tim Horton in a full blizzard just for coffee and donuts.  Others blame the exchange rate which is not substantially different than it has been for a number of years.  We are now travelling to Europe and Asia for vacations and that is definitely not cheap.  We are making new habits and traditions instead of same old same old vacationing in a country that has shown us clearly that you are not our friends.  Your country just wants our money.

We have been deeply saddened to realize that it was only our people who thought we were great friends who look out for each other and stand up for each other when insulted or threatened by other countries.  

The North remembers.  We may not learn this lesson as quickly as we should but we do learn and we will not forget.

Canada has many friends and we are now growing much closer to them.  Middle powers are realizing our true worth.

Keep elbows up!  🇨🇦💪😠

text: There is only a very small group of foreign tourists who are so political that the dislike of a particular government would deter them from travel. However, when there is a sense that those government's policies could directly impact their journey that changes drastically. Perhaps there is a non-neglegible risk that you are rejected at the border, forfeiting your hotel booking, never mind the transatlantic flight in vain. The erraticness of some of Trump's policies is also an issue: perhaps your visa-free status is revoked all of a sudden and you are supposed to fork out for an expenditure that was not on your bingo card. Producing your 5-year-history of social media was an idea floating in the air, seriously? What would that even entail for the permanently off-line? Besides politics, there were also stories of part of the US tourism industry fleecing world cup travellers, e.g. New Jersey train ticket prices going up 5-fold.

author: @AntinousXIII
text:
As an Afrikaner from Johannesburg, I've felt since I was a kid that we live in gilded cages. I could never understand why so many South Africans say "it's not so bad here," because to me it always felt deeply wrong and unnatural - driving from one secure area to the next. Especially when compared to the stories my grandparents and parents told about life in South Africa when they were my age in the old days.

It was only when I grew up that I realised why people here say those things: they're woefully and blindly optimistic because they really have no other option. They've made their peace with this way of living, because this is their home. It's not easy to just pack up and leave. So they cope by convincing themselves that our circumstances aren't "that bad" - and it's just so damn wrong.

I recently visited Austria, leaving the country for the first time, and the one thing I couldn't wrap my head around was being able to walk around freely at night without fearing for my safety or being distrustful of the people I passed on the streets. I constantly had this feeling that what I was doing might be dangerous. I even asked the person I was with if it was safe to be walking around the city so late at night. They laughed and thought it was hilarious. They genuinely couldn't understand my anxiety.

It's heartbreaking that this is what our country is now - and it most definitely is NOT normal. 
Loved the video - Dankie, Callum ^^

author: @ninny65
text:
This is quite interesting, shedding light on what the Apartheid government were trying to do, even though it could be seen also as an attempt to maintain federal control by giving devolving power to ethnic groups. It's ironic too, because even in modern day South Africa as we speak, there is movements attempting to separate the different black ethnic groups by Non-European descend peoples. We can see this with Operation Dudula and the March and March Movement. Data says roughly 26% of South Africans identify more strongly with their ethnic group than with their national identity, a sharp increase from previous decades. Only about 16% of citizens now value their national identity over their ethnicity. Only 31% of South Africans reporting that they trust members of other ethnic groups "somewhat" or "a lot". More than 40% of South Africans believe the government discriminates against their specific ethnic group. 

The political movement for the "Rainbow Nation" seems to not be as watertight as some might have hoped for. I think the problem is the movement was more aspirational than a revolution on rejecting tribal/ethnic identity. And politically speaking, simply a means to shift power away from the white minority

text:
The problem with this is that the extent to which South Africa was a developed country previously is questionable.  

It had a capitalist economy based on resource extraction - the famous example was diamond extraction.  That wealth was shared amongst the ruling class in apartheid and used to keep that political system going for a few decades.  When it collapsed, everyone who was kept in poverty all that time had many reasons to force through both pleasant and unpleasant changes.   

I'm afraid this is a "your grandparents caused this" situation.

author: @Asmarman3
text:
L'objectif n'est pas de se qualifier pour la Coupe du monde, mais de concourir pour le titre et de battre les grandes équipes. Les équipes arabes et africains sont loin de cela, à l'exception du Maroc, toujours premier dans tous les disciplines notamment de l' athlétisme du Aouita, et motawakil en 84 passant par le hicham el garouje salah hissou, bakali...la liste est longue.
En foot, le premier point pour les Arabes et l' Afrique dès leur première participation en 1970. En 1986, ils passent le premier tour à se qualifier en tant que leader du  groupe relevé. Enfin, ils sont les premiers à se qualifier pour les demi-finales de la Coupe du monde au Qatar en 2022. 
Aucune équipe arabe et africaine qui ne leur ressemble aujourd'hui, surtout depuis 2018. Le Maroc est le visage du football africain et arabe, non seulement parmi les grandes équipes, mais dans toutes les catégories : 
champion d'Afrique U17 à deux reprises et demi-finaliste de la dernière Coupe du monde en 2023. 
Champion d'Afrique U20, est désormais champion du monde en 2025 au Chili.  
champion d'Afrique U23, et médaillés de bronze olympiques en 2024 à Paris, 
l'équipe locale, triple championne d'Afrique d' affilié (2018, 2020 et 2024). 
Et enfin, l'équipe senior, quatrième au monde en demi-finale en 2022.
 Sans oublier les équipes féminines et de futsal, où le Maroc domine l'Afrique et le monde arabe avec sept coupes consécutives… Le Maroc est le seul pays à avoir d'avantages des talons dans tous les championnats continentaux et mondiaux…
